quote: its collapse may well lead to an AI winter of sorts, like what happened in the mid-1980s
claim: The collapse of GenAI may lead to an AI winter similar to the mid-1980s expert systems bust

quote: What the collapse of generative AI might lead to, after a quiet period, is a renaissance.
claim: After a quiet period following the GenAI collapse, a renaissance in AI using better techniques will emerge

quote: The biggest intrinsic failings of generative AI have to do with reliability, in a way that I believe can never be solved, given their inherent nature.
claim: The reliability problems of generative AI are inherent to the approach and can never be fully solved

quote: At some level, generative AI is a bit like (though not exactly like) a lookup table
claim: Generative AI functions approximately like a lookup table, good at retrieving trained-on patterns but poor at generalizing beyond them

quote: they are better at things that they have encountered before than things that they haven't. They are especially poor if new items are different in important and subtle ways from old ones.
claim: LLMs perform better on inputs similar to their training data and are especially poor when inputs differ in subtle but important ways

quote: LLMs are simply never going to work reliably, at least not in the general form that so many people last year seemed to be hoping
claim: LLMs will never work reliably as general-purpose systems

quote: the deepest problem is that LLMs literally can't sanity-check their own work
claim: LLMs cannot sanity-check their own work, which is their deepest structural limitation

quote: they are simply and true next-word predictors (or, as I once put it, "autocomplete on steroids"), with no inherent way of verifying whether their predictions are correct
claim: LLMs are next-word predictors with no inherent mechanism for verifying the correctness of their outputs

quote: any "reasoning" or "planning" in LLMs is hit or miss, viable if the particulars of a given situation are close enough to what is in the training set, but highly vulnerable if they are not
claim: LLM reasoning and planning is unreliable: it works when situations resemble training data but fails when they do not

quote: Hallucinations and boneheaded errors of reasoning and what I like to call discomprehensions are, as far as I can tell, an inevitable byproduct of LLMs
claim: Hallucinations, reasoning errors, and discomprehensions are an inevitable byproduct of the LLM architecture

quote: Since LLMs inevitably hallucinate and are constitutionally incapable of checking their own work, there are really only two possibilities: we abandon them, or we use them as components in larger systems that can reason and plan better
claim: LLMs must either be abandoned or used as components in larger neurosymbolic systems that can reason and plan

quote: two of the most dominant (and in my humble opinion most misguided) figures in AI, Geoffrey Hinton and Yann LeCun have, for various reasons never fully explained, fought this potential approach
claim: Hinton and LeCun have been misguided in opposing neurosymbolic approaches to AI

quote: OpenAI has for the most part stuck to Hinton and LeCun's dogma, focusing the far greater part of its efforts on "scaling" LLMs (making them bigger and bigger with more data), and trying hard to eschew symbols and symbolic rules
claim: OpenAI has dogmatically focused on scaling LLMs while eschewing symbolic reasoning approaches

quote: all the major tech companies are building exactly the same thing (bigger and bigger LLMs based on more data), achieving almost exactly the same results (a passel of GPT-4 level models, hardly different from each other, all struggling with hallucinations and boneheaded errors), and hardly any investment in anything else
claim: Major AI companies are all building nearly identical LLMs with nearly identical results and nearly identical flaws, with minimal investment in alternative approaches

quote: GDM's new work is powerful proof of concept (pun intended!). Neural networks and symbol systems can be profitably combined, contra Hinton and LeCun.
claim: Google DeepMind's AlphaProof and AlphaGeometry results prove that neural networks and symbolic systems can be profitably combined

quote: neurosymbolic approaches themselves aren't a panacea or magic bullet—much more is required, including an infrastructure of knowledge a way of deriving cognitive models from inputs such as text and video
claim: Neurosymbolic approaches are necessary but not sufficient; they also require knowledge infrastructure and cognitive model derivation from inputs

quote: expecting AI to "solve" AGI without "System II" mechanisms for symbol-manipulation is like expecting bears to solve quantum mechanics. There can be no path to AGI without neurosymbolic AI.
claim: There is no path to AGI without neurosymbolic AI incorporating System II symbolic reasoning

quote: Many coders and tech aficionados may love ChatGPT for work, but much of the outside world feels quite differently.
claim: GenAI is popular among coders and tech enthusiasts but most non-technical workers find it unhelpful or counterproductive

trigger: Multiple negative GenAI news reports in one week
quote: I have long argued that Generative AI might be a dud; I just didn't expect it to fade so fast.
claim: Generative AI is fading commercially faster than expected

trigger: Multiple negative GenAI news reports in one week
quote: The bubble has begun to burst. Users have lost faith, clients have lost faith, VC's have lost faith.
claim: The GenAI bubble has begun to burst as of late July 2024, with users, clients, and VCs losing faith

trigger: Multiple negative GenAI news reports in one week
quote: The fact that the GenAI bubble is apparently bursting sooner than expected may soon free up resources for other approaches, e.g., into neurosymbolic AI
claim: The bursting of the GenAI bubble may free up resources for alternative approaches like neurosymbolic AI

quote: current approaches to machine learning (which underlies most of the AI people talk about today) are lousy at outliers
claim: Current machine learning approaches are fundamentally poor at handling outliers -- inputs that differ significantly from training data

quote: Machine learning had trouble with outliers then, and it still does.
claim: Machine learning's inability to handle outliers has persisted for over 30 years and remains unsolved

quote: An entire industry has been built - and will collapse - because people aren't getting it.
claim: The GenAI industry will collapse because most participants do not understand that current ML is fundamentally poor at outliers

quote: The people who have temporarily gotten rich or famous on AI have done so by pretending that this outlier problem simply doesn't exist, or that a remedy for it is imminent.
claim: AI industry leaders have gotten rich by pretending the outlier problem does not exist or that a fix is imminent

quote: The "localism" in how neural networks are trained makes that an inherent problem.
claim: The local nature of neural network training makes poor outlier generalization an inherent and unsolvable problem within the neural network paradigm

quote: Neural networks don't have a good way of acquiring sufficiently general abstractions.
claim: Neural networks lack the ability to acquire sufficiently general abstractions needed for reliable performance on novel inputs

quote: Symbolic systems have always been good for outliers; neural networks have always struggled with them.
claim: Symbolic systems handle outliers well while neural networks struggle with them, making hybrid approaches necessary

quote: You can't think we are close to AGI once you realize that as yet we have no general solution to the outlier problem. You just can't.
claim: AGI is not close because there is no general solution to the outlier problem in neural networks

quote: everything that people like Altman and Musk and Kurzweil are currently saying about AGI being nigh seems like sheer fantasy, on par with imagining that really tall ladders will soon make it to the moon
claim: Claims by Altman, Musk, and Kurzweil that AGI is imminent are sheer fantasy given the unsolved outlier problem

quote: Generative AI, at least we know it know, doesn't actually work that well, and maybe never will.
claim: Generative AI does not work well and may never work well

quote: the collapse of the generative AI bubble – in a financial sense – appears imminent, likely before the end of the calendar year
claim: The GenAI financial bubble will likely collapse before the end of 2024

quote: investors may well stop forking out money at the rates they have, enthusiasm may diminish, and a lot of people may lose their shirts
claim: GenAI investment rates will slow, enthusiasm will diminish, and many investors will lose significant money

quote: Companies that are currently valued at billions of dollars may fold, or stripped for parts. Few of last year's darlings will ever meet recent expectations
claim: Some billion-dollar GenAI companies will fold or be stripped for parts, and few will meet their recent valuation expectations

quote: Things may look radically different by the end of 2024 from how they looked just a few months ago.
claim: The GenAI industry landscape will look radically different by the end of 2024 compared to early 2024

trigger: Nvidia stock drop, OpenAI GPT-5 delay, Elon suing OpenAI, Google antitrust loss
quote: LLMs are not AGI, and (on their own) never will be; scaling alone was never going to be enough. The only mystery was what would happen when the big players realized that the jig was up, and that scaling was not in fact "All You Need".
claim: LLMs will never be AGI on their own and scaling alone was never going to be sufficient

trigger: Nvidia stock drop, OpenAI GPT-5 delay, Elon suing OpenAI, Google antitrust loss
quote: it is also not out of the question that today could end someday end up being seen as a turning point
claim: August 5, 2024 could be seen as a turning point in the deflation of the GenAI bubble

quote: OpenAI could wind up being seen as the WeWork of AI. Huge operating losses, big staff expenses, an apparent morale problem, no GPT-5 yet, competitors catching up, Meta giving away similar tech for free, Microsoft cutting deals with competitors.
claim: OpenAI could become the WeWork of AI due to huge losses, morale problems, no GPT-5, competitors catching up, and free alternatives from Meta

quote: Will they earn enough to justify their $80B valuation? Raise a future round at even higher valuation, when the money runs out? Prospects don't seem as strong as they once did.
claim: OpenAI's prospects for justifying its $80B valuation or raising future rounds are weakening

quote: The board, which basically said it couldn't trust Sam, may have had a point.
claim: The OpenAI board that tried to fire Sam Altman over trust issues may have been justified

trigger: OpenAI webcam investment and data collection practices
quote: Altman wants to know - and monetize - everything about you.
claim: Sam Altman is pursuing a strategy to collect and monetize comprehensive personal data about users

trigger: Fei-Fei Li Fortune letter opposing SB-1047
quote: SB-1047 does not require predicting every use
claim: California SB-1047 does not require AI developers to predict every possible use of their model; it focuses on specific critical harms

trigger: Fei-Fei Li Fortune letter opposing SB-1047
quote: it focuses on specific, serious "critical harms" such as mass casualties, weapons of mass destruction, large-scale cyberattacks, and AI models autonomously committing serious felonies. Those seem reasonable to me
claim: SB-1047's focus on critical harms such as mass casualties, WMDs, large-scale cyberattacks, and autonomous felonies is reasonable

trigger: Fei-Fei Li Fortune letter opposing SB-1047
quote: the "kill switch" requirement doesn't apply to open-source models once they are out of the original developer's control
claim: SB-1047's kill switch requirement does not apply to open-source models once released from the developer's control

trigger: Fei-Fei Li Fortune letter opposing SB-1047
quote: much of the bill's requirements are limited to models with training runs of $100 million+. Companies that can afford that, presumably valued in the billions, are not exactly "little-tech."
claim: SB-1047 requirements primarily apply to models costing $100M+ to train, which means they affect large companies, not little tech or academia

trigger: Fei-Fei Li Fortune letter opposing SB-1047
quote: asking for standards is not unique to AI; it's common across many industries to ask that companies evaluate the safety of their products according to set standards
claim: Requiring safety standards for AI is not unusual; many industries (pharma, aviation, automobiles) already have analogous product safety requirements

quote: I foresee a significant reframing of expectations
claim: The AI industry will undergo a significant reframing of expectations about what current AI can deliver

quote: no, GPT-5 did not drop this week as many had hoped
claim: GPT-5 had not been released as of August 2024 despite widespread expectations

quote: The bill no longer allows the [Attorney General] to sue companies for negligent safety practices before a catastrophic event occurs
claim: The amended SB-1047 was significantly weakened by removing the AG's ability to sue before a catastrophic event, removing the new oversight agency, removing perjury requirements for safety certifications, and weakening the standard from reasonable assurance to reasonable care

quote: Sam Altman stood next to me in the Senate, he told the Senate that he strongly favored AI regulation. But behind the scenes his team fought to water down the EU AI Act
claim: OpenAI publicly favors AI regulation while privately working to weaken it, as seen in their lobbying against the EU AI Act and SB-1047

quote: it's too narrow, focused mainly on catastrophic harm that causes over a half a billion dollars damaage, and does too little to address other issues
claim: SB-1047 is too narrow in scope, focused only on catastrophic harm exceeding $500M in damage, and does not address misinformation, discrimination, deepfakes, environmental impacts, or workforce displacement

quote: we need federal legislation; a messy state-by-state patchwork is one thing that could actually stifle AI development
claim: The US needs federal AI legislation; a patchwork of state-by-state laws could actually stifle AI development

quote: we will see, contra Andreessen Horowitz (aka a16z) and others in Silicon Valley, that life goes on — that innovation will continue just fine, that no major tech company actually leaves California, and that modest regulation that encourages companies to do the right thing is not fatal to the industry
claim: If SB-1047 passes, innovation will continue, no major tech company will leave California, and modest AI regulation will not be fatal to the industry

quote: there is not one risk but many: bias, disinformation, defamation, toxicity, invasion of privacy, and so much more, to say nothing of risks to employment, intellectual property, and the environment
claim: AI poses many distinct risks including bias, disinformation, defamation, toxicity, privacy invasion, employment disruption, IP theft, and environmental harm, requiring a comprehensive regulatory approach

quote: self-regulation is never going to work
claim: Self-regulation of AI companies will never work

quote: Companies like OpenAI that have talked big about investing in safety research and then stinted on their promises may finally be incentivitized to walk the walk
claim: OpenAI has talked about investing in safety research but failed to follow through on those promises

trigger: William Saunders going on record about OpenAI
quote: promises have been made and not kept; they lost faith in Altman personally, and have lost faith in the company's commitment to AI safety
claim: Multiple former OpenAI employees report that promises on AI safety have not been kept and they have lost faith in Altman and in OpenAI's commitment to safety

trigger: William Saunders going on record about OpenAI
quote: Current AI is not all that scary, but future AI might become very scary. We don't know when that will happen, but we are not prepared for it.
claim: Current AI is not very dangerous, but future AI could become very dangerous, and society is not prepared for that eventuality

trigger: William Saunders going on record about OpenAI
quote: Internal governance is key; it shouldn't be just one person at the top of one company calling the shots for all humanity
claim: AI governance should not allow a single individual at the top of a company to make potentially species-defining decisions without oversight

trigger: William Saunders going on record about OpenAI
quote: nothing legally prevents it. Boards can be weak, and may not even be consulted; rank-and-file employees rarely have much say
claim: Nothing in current US law prevents a single CEO from making potentially catastrophic AI deployment decisions unilaterally, without board or employee input

trigger: William Saunders going on record about OpenAI
quote: companies should not be able to make decisions of potentially enormous magnitude on their own; external advisory bodies should have a strong say in weighing risks versus benefits
claim: External governance bodies should have a strong say in AI companies' decisions of potentially enormous magnitude

trigger: William Saunders going on record about OpenAI
quote: if we don't figure out the governance problem, internal and external, before the next big AI advance—whenever it may come—we could be in serious trouble
claim: If AI governance problems (both internal and external) are not solved before the next major AI advance, society could face serious trouble

trigger: William Saunders going on record about OpenAI
quote: With companies accountable to nothing, not even what they have said to Congress and the White House, self-regulation is never going to work
claim: AI self-regulation is ineffective because companies are not accountable to their own public commitments, including those made to Congress and the White House

trigger: William Saunders going on record about OpenAI
quote: one of the most important reasons for passing SB-1047 in California was its whistleblower protections
claim: SB-1047's whistleblower protections are among its most important provisions for enabling AI safety accountability

trigger: William Saunders going on record about OpenAI
quote: Saunders and I disagree on timelines. He thinks it is at least somewhat plausible we will see AGI in a few years; I do not.
claim: Marcus does not think it is plausible that AGI will arrive within a few years

quote: AI, which after all, is likely to change the world in coming years, affecting virtually every aspect of society, from employment to education to healthcare to national defense
claim: AI is likely to change the world in coming years, affecting employment, education, healthcare, and national defense

quote: AI contributes to escalation in both
claim: AI is contributing to escalating fraud and disinformation

quote: Our long-term future may actually be shaped more by AI policy than tariffs
claim: Long-term societal outcomes may be shaped more by AI policy than by trade and tariff policy

quote: Still flawed, still limited, seem more impressive on first use
claim: OpenAI's o1 model is still flawed, still limited, and seems more impressive on first use than on sustained evaluation, the same pattern Marcus predicted for GPT-4

quote: the new system appears to depend heavily on synthetic data, and that such data may be easier to produce in some domains (such as those in which o1 is most successful, like some aspects of math) than others
claim: OpenAI o1 depends heavily on synthetic data, which is easier to produce in some domains like math than others, limiting its generalizability

quote: The more things change, the more they remain the same
claim: Despite new AI model releases, the fundamental pattern of initial impressiveness followed by revealed limitations remains unchanged

quote: GPT-5 hasn't dropped, Sora hasn't shipped, the company had an operating loss of $5b last year, there is no obvious moat, Meta is giving away similar software for free, many lawsuits pending
claim: As of September 2024, OpenAI has not shipped GPT-5 or Sora, lost $5B in operations, has no obvious moat, faces free competition from Meta, and has many pending lawsuits

quote: Yet people are valuing this company at $150 billion dollars. Absolutely insane.
claim: OpenAI's $150 billion valuation is not justified given its $5B operating losses, departing talent, missing product milestones, and lack of moat

quote: Investors shouldn't be pouring more money at higher valuations, they should be asking what is going on
claim: Investors in OpenAI should be questioning the company's direction rather than investing at higher valuations

quote: They likely need that money quite badly – especially since their main strategy for improving models is simply to make them bigger, a lot bigger, hence a lot more expensive
claim: OpenAI urgently needs its funding because its main strategy is scaling models larger, which is extremely expensive

quote: It is likely that they lose money on every use query, once training costs are factored in
claim: OpenAI likely loses money on every user query when training costs are factored in

quote: if they don't get a fresh infusion of cash they could run out of money before they do
claim: Without fresh funding, OpenAI could run out of money before shipping GPT-5 or Sora

quote: OpenAI is in many ways the poster child of the Generative AI revolution. If they stumble, it will have ripple effects
claim: If OpenAI stumbles financially, it will cause ripple effects across the entire generative AI industry, affecting Nvidia valuations and other companies' financing terms

quote: Nobody should able to make that switch for free, at no cost, or people will exploit nonprofit rules infinitely, going forward
claim: OpenAI's transition from nonprofit to for-profit should not be allowed to happen at no cost, as that would create a precedent for exploiting nonprofit rules

quote: We spent 18 months hearing about how Generative AI was going to "10x" coding, improving programmer productivity by a factor of 10. The data are coming in – and it's not.
claim: Generative AI is not delivering 10x improvement in programmer productivity; the data shows it is not happening

quote: One result with 800 programmers shows little improvement and more bugs
claim: A study of 800 programmers using GenAI coding tools showed little productivity improvement and more bugs

quote: Another somewhat more positive study doesn't look at bugs, but shows moderate (26%, not 1000%) improvement for junior developers, and only "marginal gains" for senior developers
claim: Studies show GenAI coding tools provide at most 26% improvement for junior developers and only marginal gains for senior developers, far from the claimed 10x

quote: An earlier study on gitclear showed "downward pressure on code quality"
claim: GenAI coding tools create downward pressure on code quality

quote: Yet another earlier study (with earlier models) showed evidence of users writing less secure code
claim: GenAI coding assistance leads to users writing less secure code, which could cause net productivity loss long-term

quote: The tracks here are pointing to modest improvements, with some potentials costs for security and technical debt, not 10x improvement
claim: The empirical evidence on GenAI coding tools points to modest improvements with potential costs in security and technical debt, not 10x improvement

quote: A good IDE (versus none) probably is a much bigger, much less expensive, much less hyped improvement that helps more people more reliably
claim: A good IDE provides a bigger, cheaper, and more reliable productivity improvement for programmers than GenAI coding tools

quote: 10x-ing requires deep conceptual understanding – exactly what GenAI lacks. Writing lines of code without that understanding can only help so much.
claim: Achieving 10x coding productivity requires deep conceptual understanding, which GenAI lacks; writing code without understanding can only help marginally

trigger: Eric Schmidt remarks on AI energy at Future Investment Initiative
quote: Almost every putative virtue of AI (aside from making a small number of people rich) has been promissory. We have been told that AI will "solve physics" (per Sam Altman), make major breakthroughs in medicine, battery technology, etc, but so far none of that has materialized.
claim: Almost every claimed societal benefit of AI (solving physics, medical breakthroughs, battery technology) remains unfulfilled promises; only enriching a small number of people has materialized

trigger: Eric Schmidt remarks on AI energy at Future Investment Initiative
quote: The case for techno-utopia, especially with current technology —which struggles with reasoning in open-ended domains, especially in rare situations with smaller amounts of specific data — is so far very abstract and vague
claim: Current AI technology struggles with reasoning in open-ended domains, especially in rare situations with limited specific data, making the case for AI-driven techno-utopia very weak

trigger: Eric Schmidt remarks on AI energy at Future Investment Initiative
quote: The only potent trick anyone seems to have is scaling, and the more scaling the big tech companies do, the more power they will consume
claim: The AI industry's primary improvement approach is scaling, which causes power consumption to grow proportionally

trigger: Eric Schmidt remarks on AI energy at Future Investment Initiative
quote: GenAI, which is consuming all the power, probably isn't going to do a whole lot for addressing climate change, when it can't even reason reliably about river crossing problems
claim: GenAI, which consumes massive power, is unlikely to help address climate change because it cannot reason reliably even about simple problems

trigger: Eric Schmidt remarks on AI energy at Future Investment Initiative
quote: LLMs are not the AI we need to address climate change; they are the AI we would use if we wanted to risk serious harm to the climate
claim: LLMs are not the type of AI needed to address climate change; more domain-specific and specialized AI would be needed for that

trigger: Eric Schmidt remarks on AI energy at Future Investment Initiative
quote: we can't, or at least shouldn't, place massive bets like these without transparency and accountability
claim: Society should not place massive bets on AI's environmental costs without transparency, accountability, and independent scientific evaluation

trigger: Eric Schmidt remarks on AI energy at Future Investment Initiative
quote: profit-driven companies with unelected leaders shouldn't decide on their own, for all of us. Society has to have a vote here
claim: Profit-driven AI companies with unelected leaders should not be making decisions about planetary-scale environmental tradeoffs unilaterally; society must have a say

trigger: Eric Schmidt remarks on AI energy at Future Investment Initiative
quote: Chatbots and movie synthesis machines (perhaps the most energetically costly systems) are fun, maybe even amazing - but I don't think we should be risking our planet for them
claim: The environmental costs of chatbots and media synthesis AI systems do not justify the planetary risk they pose

trigger: Eric Schmidt remarks on AI energy at Future Investment Initiative
quote: Diverting funding from them to more focused efforts around special-purpose AI addressing climate change might make more sense
claim: Redirecting funding from large generative AI systems to special-purpose AI for climate change would be a better investment

trigger: 2024 Nobel Prizes for AI (Hinton Physics, Hassabis Chemistry)
quote: the anti-neurosymbolic tradition is limited; its most visible manifestation, LLMs, has brought fame and money, but no robust solution to solving any particular problem with great reliability
claim: LLMs, the most visible product of the pure neural network tradition, have not produced a robust solution to any particular problem with great reliability

trigger: 2024 Nobel Prizes for AI (Hinton Physics, Hassabis Chemistry)
quote: It remains opaque, hobbled with hallucinations and bone-headed errors, and greedy to the extreme in its need for electricity, water, and data
claim: LLMs remain opaque, prone to hallucinations and basic errors, and extremely resource-intensive in electricity, water, and data requirements

trigger: 2024 Nobel Prizes for AI (Hinton Physics, Hassabis Chemistry)
quote: It is, as far as I can tell, the first Nobel Prize for Neurosymbolic AI
claim: AlphaFold2 is a neurosymbolic AI system that combines neural networks with classical symbolic machinery, and its Chemistry Nobel is the first Nobel Prize for neurosymbolic AI

trigger: 2024 Nobel Prizes for AI (Hinton Physics, Hassabis Chemistry)
quote: It's time for a new approach, and Hassabis sees that. His open-mindedness will serve the field well.
claim: The AI field needs to move beyond pure deep learning toward neurosymbolic approaches like those used by Hassabis in AlphaFold2

trigger: 2024 Nobel Prizes for AI (Hinton Physics, Hassabis Chemistry)
quote: Hinton has long stood for back-propagation (or something similar all the way down); in recent decades, he has ridiculed neurosymbolic AI
claim: Hinton's dogmatic opposition to neurosymbolic AI has been limiting for the field, in contrast to Hassabis's more open-minded approach

quote: we found no evidence of formal reasoning in language models …. Their behavior is better explained by sophisticated pattern matching—so fragile, in fact, that changing names can alter results by ~10%!
claim: LLMs do not perform formal reasoning; their behavior is better explained by sophisticated pattern matching that is fragile enough that changing names alters results by approximately 10%

quote: There is just no way can you build reliable agents on this foundation, where changing a word or two in irrelevant ways or adding a few bit of irrelevant info can give you a different answer
claim: Reliable AI agents cannot be built on LLM foundations because minor irrelevant changes to inputs can produce different outputs

quote: Performance is ok on small problems, but quickly tails off
claim: LLM performance on reasoning tasks degrades significantly as problem size increases, unlike systems with true formal reasoning capabilities

quote: Elon Musk's putative robotaxis are likely to suffer from a similar affliction: they may well work safely for the most common situations, but are also likely struggle to reason abstractly enough in some circumstances
claim: Tesla's robotaxis will likely work in common situations but struggle in uncommon circumstances requiring abstract reasoning, due to the same limitations as LLMs

quote: I strongly believe the current results are robust. After a quarter century of "real soon now" promissory notes I would want a lot more than hand-waving to be convinced than at an LLM-compatible solution is in reach
claim: LLMs' inability to do formal reasoning is a robust finding, and there is no credible evidence that an LLM-compatible solution to this problem is within reach

quote: symbol manipulation, in which some knowledge is represented truly abstractly in terms of variables and operations over those variables, much as we see in algebra and traditional computer programming, must be part of the mix
claim: Symbol manipulation with truly abstract variable representation must be part of any AI system capable of formal reasoning; neurosymbolic AI is likely a necessary condition for progress

quote: even after years of them, the tech industry has nothing like a robust response
claim: Despite years of jailbreak attacks on LLMs, the tech industry has no robust defense against them

quote: In hindsight, ChatGPT may come to be seen as the greatest publicity stunt in AI history, an intoxicating glimpse at a future that may actually take years to realize
claim: ChatGPT may be remembered as the greatest publicity stunt in AI history, offering a glimpse of a future that will take years to actually realize

quote: companies like Google, Tesla, and Figure.AI are now stuffing jailbreak-vulnerable LLMs into robots
claim: Companies are deploying jailbreak-vulnerable LLMs into physical robots, creating physical security risks

quote: By empowering LLM companies and putting almost no effective guardrails in place, we as a society are asking for trouble
claim: Society is inviting trouble by empowering LLM companies while putting almost no effective guardrails in place

quote: we now have the world's most used chatbot, governed by training data that nobody knows about, obeying an algorithm that is only hinted at, glorified by the media, and yet with ethical guardrails that only sorta kinda work
claim: Leading chatbots have opaque training data, opaque algorithms, media glorification, and ethical guardrails that only partially work

quote: OpenAI might be screaming success - but its success is hardly assured
claim: OpenAI's success is not assured despite its public image of triumph

quote: They are burning a ton of money, competition has greatly increased, turnover has been considerable
claim: OpenAI faces serious headwinds: burning large amounts of money, greatly increased competition, and considerable employee turnover

quote: their relationship with Microsoft is fraying
claim: OpenAI's relationship with Microsoft is fraying as of October 2024

quote: The new model o1 is interesting, but expensive to operate, and far from a general solution. There is a reason OpenAI didn't choose to dub it GPT-5.
claim: OpenAI's o1 model is expensive to operate and far from a general solution, which is why OpenAI did not brand it as GPT-5

quote: Without Microsoft's unwavering support, they could find themselves in deep trouble. Living up to OpenAI's $150B valuation won't be easy.
claim: OpenAI could face deep trouble without continued Microsoft support, and living up to its $150B valuation will be very difficult

quote: When I think of OpenAI, I often think of WeWork: charismatic founder, immense valuation, questionable business plan, and the possibility of similar immense deflation in their valuation, if confidence wavers
claim: OpenAI shares key characteristics with WeWork: charismatic founder, immense valuation, questionable business plan, and potential for dramatic valuation collapse if confidence wavers

quote: the graph "is a fantasy about the future, and not at all obvious that the "data" plotted correspond to anything real. The absence of label on the Y axis maybe tells you all you need to know."
claim: An OpenAI employee's graph of AI capability progress has no labeled Y axis and does not appear to plot real data, presenting made-up projections as if they were empirical

quote: I do not know any measure that pegs the delta between GPT-4 and o1 (marked as "today") as being triple the delta between GPT-3 and GPT-4
claim: There is no known measure by which the improvement from GPT-4 to o1 is triple the improvement from GPT-3 to GPT-4; the gap from GPT-3 to GPT-4 was by all accounts much larger

quote: the whole thing is premised on OpenAI winning the race to AGI and soon — without a clear, feasible technological path there (unless you count "scaling" which sounds like "hope, without a moat" to me)
claim: OpenAI's valuation is premised on winning the race to AGI soon, but there is no clear, feasible technological path to get there; scaling alone is not a credible strategy

quote: If the answer isn't bigger LLMs, we may have wasted half a decade
claim: If bigger LLMs turn out not to be the path to AGI, the AI industry may have wasted half a decade of investment and effort

quote: neither OpenAI nor any other frontier lab is ready, and the world is also not ready
claim: Neither OpenAI nor any other frontier AI lab is ready for AGI, and the world is also not ready

quote: Brundage was one of the last of an old guard of AI safety and governance employees of the AI company. His departure is yet another sign that OpenAI, as we knew it, is dead
claim: The original safety-focused OpenAI is effectively dead, with most of its safety and governance leaders having departed

quote: more than half of the employees focused on AGI safety had left the company in just the previous few months
claim: More than half of OpenAI's employees focused on AGI safety left the company in the months before August 2024

quote: safety culture and processes have taken a backseat to shiny products
claim: OpenAI's safety culture and processes have been deprioritized in favor of shipping products, according to departing safety team lead Jan Leike

quote: the Superalignment team never got the 20 percent of compute it was promised
claim: OpenAI's Superalignment team never received the 20% of compute resources it was promised

quote: it required outgoing employees to sign draconian, lifetime non-disparagement and non-disclosure agreements to retain their vested equity
claim: OpenAI required departing employees to sign lifetime non-disparagement and NDA agreements to keep their vested equity, a draconian practice in place since at least 2019

quote: I think the next major fault line in the AI debates will be posture toward China
claim: The next major fault line in AI policy debates will be whether to compete or cooperate with China on AI development

quote: Existential clash of civilizations thinking motivated the most dangerous choices ever made, from the nuclear arms buildup to the Soviets' illegal, secret bioweapons program
claim: Framing AI development as an existential US-China competition is dangerous because similar zero-sum thinking historically led to the nuclear arms race and Soviet bioweapons programs

quote: we should be very wary of letting science guys with toy models of the world dictate international relations
claim: AI researchers and tech leaders should not be allowed to dictate international relations based on simplistic models of geopolitical competition

quote: Despite a long record of rhetorically supporting regulation, OpenAI formally opposed the first major binding AI safety bill in the US, California's SB 1047
claim: OpenAI formally opposed SB-1047, the first major binding US AI safety bill, despite a long record of publicly supporting AI regulation

quote: I think by 2040 probably there are more humanoid robots than there are people
claim: Musk's prediction that humanoid robots will outnumber humans by 2040 is wildly improbable; there are only 1.5 billion cars and Roomba has sold only 50 million units at a few hundred dollars each

quote: it's just fantasy to imagine selling 200 times as many humanoid robots in the nearish term when nobody knows how to build a single safe, reliable, generally useful humanoid right now, at any price
claim: Nobody currently knows how to build a single safe, reliable, generally useful humanoid robot at any price

quote: I feel comfortable saying that that AI is getting 10 times better each year
claim: Musk's claim that AI is getting 10x better each year is unsupported; o1 is not 100x better than GPT-4 by any measure

quote: I think it will be able to do anything that any human can do, possibly within the next year or two
claim: Musk's claim that AI will match all human capabilities within one to two years is nonsense; Marcus offers to bet a million dollars against it

quote: "10,000 times smarter than humans"? What does that even mean? It's a made up round number, with no real meaning; pure hype
claim: Masayoshi Son's claim of 10,000 times smarter than humans ASI by 2035 is a meaningless made-up number and pure hype

quote: quantitative vibes are still just vibes
claim: Precise-sounding quantitative predictions about AI progress (10x per year, 10,000x smarter) are unfounded vibes, not data-driven projections

quote: Any serious scientist or engineer knows you can't possibly predict the future with that kind of precision, especially when there are so many unknowns
claim: AI progress cannot be predicted with the precision claimed by figures like Musk and Son, given the many unknowns in materials costs, battery science, software quality, and model efficiency

quote: the audience of investors at the forum was being lied to: they were being assured that everything in the field of AI is under control and well plotted out when in fact science is hard and the field is in its infancy
claim: AI industry leaders are misleading investors by presenting AI development as predictable and well-plotted when the field is actually in its infancy with hard, unsolved scientific problems

quote: If they can find a plausible story to invest a huge amount of other people's money in, they make huge fees (typically 2% of whatever is invested), immediately
claim: Investors promoting massive AI investments benefit from management fees regardless of whether the investments succeed, creating a misaligned incentive structure

quote: 79% of survey correspondents said they had tried Microsoft Copilot
claim: 79% of technology executives have tried Microsoft Copilot, refuting the narrative that people are ignoring AI

quote: only 25% of the correspondents thought it was worth it
claim: Only 25% of technology executives who tried Microsoft Copilot thought it was worth it

quote: People aren't ignoring GenAI; they are waiting to see if it will work
claim: People are not ignoring generative AI; they have tried it and are waiting to see if it delivers sufficient value, with many unimpressed so far

trigger: Marc Andreessen comments and The Information reporting on Orion model
quote: deep learning is hitting a wall
claim: Deep learning and LLM scaling is hitting a wall; pure scaling will not solve hallucinations or abstraction

trigger: Marc Andreessen comments and The Information reporting on Orion model
quote: we're increasing [graphics processing units] at the same rate, we're not getting the intelligent improvements at all out of it
claim: Increasing GPU compute at the same rate is no longer yielding proportional intelligence improvements in LLMs, confirming diminishing returns from scaling

trigger: Marc Andreessen comments and The Information reporting on Orion model
quote: OpenAI's [upcoming] Orion model shows how GPT improvements are slowing down
claim: OpenAI's upcoming Orion model demonstrates that GPT improvements are slowing down

trigger: Marc Andreessen comments and The Information reporting on Orion model
quote: There is no principled solution to hallucinations in systems that traffic only in the statistics of language without explicit representation of facts and explicit tools to reason over those facts
claim: There is no principled solution to hallucinations in LLMs because they traffic only in language statistics without explicit fact representation or reasoning tools

trigger: Marc Andreessen comments and The Information reporting on Orion model
quote: LLMs will not disappear, even if improvements diminish, but the economics will likely never make sense: additional training is expensive, the more scaling, the more costly
claim: LLMs will persist but their economics will likely never make sense because additional training is expensive and scaling increases costs

trigger: Marc Andreessen comments and The Information reporting on Orion model
quote: everyone is landing in more or less the same place, which leaves nobody with a moat. LLMs such as they are, will become a commodity; price wars will keep revenue low
claim: LLMs will become a commodity with no company having a moat; price wars will keep revenue low

trigger: Marc Andreessen comments and The Information reporting on Orion model
quote: When everyone realizes this, the financial bubble may burst quickly; even NVidia might take a hit, when people realize the extent to which its valuation was based on a false premise
claim: The generative AI financial bubble may burst quickly once diminishing returns are widely recognized, and even Nvidia's valuation could suffer

trigger: Marc Andreessen comments and The Information reporting on Orion model
quote: Sky high valuation of companies like OpenAI and Microsoft are largely based on the notion that LLMs will, with continued scaling, become artificial general intelligence. As I have always warned, that's just a fantasy
claim: The high valuations of OpenAI and Microsoft are largely based on the false premise that LLM scaling will produce AGI

trigger: Marc Andreessen comments and The Information reporting on Orion model
quote: the US AI policy now, and likely in the next administration, has largely been driven by hype, and the assumption that returns for LLM scaling would not diminish
claim: US AI policy has been driven by hype and the false assumption that LLM scaling returns would not diminish

trigger: Marc Andreessen comments and The Information reporting on Orion model
quote: precious little investment has been made in other approaches. If LLMs won't get the US to trustworthy AI, and our adversaries invest in alternative approaches, we could easily be outfoxed
claim: The US has invested too little in non-LLM AI approaches; if adversaries invest in alternative approaches while the US goes all-in on LLMs, the US could be outcompeted

trigger: Marc Andreessen comments and The Information reporting on Orion model
quote: The US has been putting all its AI eggs in the LLM basket, and that may well prove to be an epic, massive mistake
claim: The US concentration of AI investment in LLMs may prove to be an epic strategic mistake

trigger: Marc Andreessen comments and The Information reporting on Orion model
quote: Reliable, trustworthy AI is surely achievable, but we may need to go back to the drawing board to get there
claim: Reliable, trustworthy AI is achievable but may require going back to the drawing board rather than continuing to scale LLMs

trigger: Marc Andreessen comments and The Information reporting on Orion model
quote: LLMs on their own will never live up to last year's wild expectations
claim: LLMs on their own will never live up to the wild expectations set in 2023

trigger: rumor of second major company hitting scaling wall
quote: scaling laws" are not physical laws. They are merely empirical generalizations that held for a certain period time, when there was enough fresh data and compute
claim: AI scaling laws are not physical laws but empirical generalizations that held only during a period of sufficient fresh data and compute

trigger: rumor of second major company hitting scaling wall
quote: there has never been any principled argument that they would solve hallucinations or reasoning, or edge cases in open-ended worlds
claim: There has never been a principled argument that scaling would solve hallucinations, reasoning failures, or edge cases in open-ended domains

trigger: rumor of second major company hitting scaling wall
quote: I strongly suspect that, contra Altman, we have in fact reached a point of diminishing returns for pure scaling
claim: Pure scaling of LLMs has reached a point of diminishing returns

trigger: rumor of second major company hitting scaling wall
quote: Just adding data and compute and training longer worked miracles for a while, but those days may well be over
claim: The era when simply adding data, compute, and training time yielded major improvements in LLMs is likely over

trigger: rumor of second major company hitting scaling wall
quote: If the rumor is verified, there could be the AI equivalent of a bank run.
claim: If multiple major AI companies confirm hitting scaling walls, it could trigger a massive loss of investor confidence resembling a bank run

trigger: study claiming ChatGPT poetry indistinguishable from Shakespeare
quote: hopes he has taught you by now to never trust the hype
claim: AI hype around capabilities like poetry generation should not be trusted at face value

quote: the so-called [LLM] scaling laws aren't universal laws like gravity but rather mere observations that might not hold forever
claim: LLM scaling laws are not universal laws but empirical observations that may not hold forever

quote: we may already [be] approaching a point of diminishing returns
claim: LLM training may already be at a point of diminishing returns

quote: Hundreds of billions of dollars were invested on the assumption that I was wrong, largely to the exclusion of investment in other approaches. Almost a half decade lost, when we could have been seriously investing in other approaches
claim: Hundreds of billions of dollars invested in pure LLM scaling came at the expense of investment in alternative AI approaches, wasting nearly half a decade

quote: symbol-manipulation, as discussed in his 2001 book The Algebraic Mind and again in the second half of "Deep learning is hitting a wall" will be part of the solution
claim: Symbol-manipulation will be part of the solution for achieving robust, trustworthy AI

trigger: Satya Nadella introducing inference-time compute scaling at Microsoft Ignite
quote: the new scaling "laws" are very much like the first – they are empirical generalizations, not physical laws. Like the first generalization, the new ones will eventually run out
claim: Inference-time compute scaling laws are empirical generalizations that will eventually run out, just like training-time scaling laws

trigger: Satya Nadella introducing inference-time compute scaling at Microsoft Ignite
quote: the alleged "scaling" law (inspired by OpenAI's o1) that Nadella emphasized— the more time you spend on a problem, the more accurate you get —is (a) expensive, because it by definition burns up more GPU time and (b) unreliable
claim: Inference-time compute scaling (as in o1) is both expensive due to GPU usage and unreliable in its improvements

trigger: Satya Nadella introducing inference-time compute scaling at Microsoft Ignite
quote: My guess is o1's test time scaling works best in closed domains where you can create a lot of reliable synthetic data (such as math problems), and not so well in open-ended domains
claim: Test-time compute scaling (as in o1) works best in closed domains amenable to synthetic data generation and poorly in open-ended domains

trigger: Satya Nadella introducing inference-time compute scaling at Microsoft Ignite
quote: GPT-4 was better than GPT-3 in almost every way; o1 is a far less uniform improvement
claim: o1 represents a far less uniform improvement over GPT-4 than GPT-4 was over GPT-3

trigger: Satya Nadella introducing inference-time compute scaling at Microsoft Ignite
quote: "Scaling" used to mean "I can predict pretty much exactly how well a given LLM will do, by knowing the size of its database and the amount of compute being used to train it, and those increases will be exponential". Now it just means "I think it will get better, not sure how much, if I can keep tweaking some parameter"
claim: The meaning of 'scaling' in AI has been devalued from precise predictive power to vague hope of improvement through parameter tweaking

trigger: Satya Nadella introducing inference-time compute scaling at Microsoft Ignite
quote: Trying to rescue LLMs by making a system that is inherently blind to factuality run longer — rather than better—is like smearing water around wet plates
claim: LLMs are inherently blind to factuality, and running them longer (inference-time scaling) does not fix this fundamental limitation

trigger: Satya Nadella introducing inference-time compute scaling at Microsoft Ignite
quote: We can and must do better. Scaling can only take us so far; the time has come for fresh invention.
claim: Scaling alone cannot achieve AI's goals; fundamentally new inventions and approaches are needed

quote: Every big tech company is desperate for training data, and we can expect more companies following the same sly opt-out playbook, hiding what is going in obscure settings and Terms of Service nobody reads
claim: Big tech companies are desperate for AI training data and will increasingly use sly opt-out tactics hidden in obscure settings to scrape user data

quote: none of us stand any chance against big tech unless we stand together
claim: Individuals cannot effectively resist big tech's data practices unless they act collectively

quote: hopes that the Trump administration will take AI regulation seriously
claim: The Trump administration should take AI regulation seriously

trigger: Microsoft response to training data scraping allegations
quote: Absent a clear statement in the Terms of Service, I personally remain uncertain
claim: Microsoft's denial that it uses Office 365 customer data for LLM training is not adequately supported by clear documentation and remains uncertain

quote: a lot of of theories about how ChatGPT would be used in practice have fizzled out
claim: Many predicted use cases for ChatGPT have failed to materialize in practice

quote: Google's search share hasn't diminished all that much
claim: ChatGPT has not significantly diminished Google's search market share despite predictions it would

quote: a lot of Fortune 500 companies are somewhat underwhelmed by the real world results they are getting
claim: Many Fortune 500 companies are underwhelmed by real-world results from generative AI

quote: There has been a ton of experimentation, but relatively few well-documented success stories
claim: Despite widespread experimentation with generative AI, there are relatively few well-documented enterprise success stories

quote: These systems have no conception of truth. Sometimes they land on it and sometimes they don't, but they're all fundamentally bullshitting
claim: LLMs have no conception of truth and are fundamentally generating plausible-sounding text without regard for factuality

quote: even today hallucinations (probably better described as confabulations) still haven't gone away. And not for lack of trying
claim: LLM hallucinations/confabulations persist despite extensive industry efforts to eliminate them

quote: The industry has tried practically everything, scaling, more scaling, and RAG. Apple even tried (to little avail) literally putting "do not hallucinate" in its system prompt. None of this has eliminated the problem.
claim: Neither scaling, RAG, nor system prompt instructions have eliminated the LLM hallucination problem

quote: Two years on, on the most important question of all – factuality and reliability — we are still pretty much where we were when ChatGPT first came out
claim: On factuality and reliability, LLMs have made little meaningful progress in the two years since ChatGPT's launch

quote: RAG, scaling, and system problems haven't eradicated the inherent tendency of LLMs to hallucinate
claim: The tendency of LLMs to hallucinate is inherent to the technology and has not been eradicated by RAG, scaling, or system prompts

quote: Commercial progress has been halting precisely because the tech simply isn't reliable
claim: The lack of commercial progress in generative AI is caused by the technology's fundamental unreliability

quote: hundreds of billions more have been invested on further speculation that scaling would somehow magically cure problems that actually appear to be inherent with the technology
claim: Problems like hallucination appear to be inherent to LLM technology rather than solvable through scaling alone

quote: neurosymbolic AI will be part of the deeper breakthroughs we need
claim: Neurosymbolic AI will be part of the deeper breakthroughs needed to advance AI beyond current LLM limitations

quote: one of the biggest applications of AI seems to be ripping off customers
claim: One of the biggest current applications of AI is being used to deny legitimate customer claims and rip off consumers

quote: AI doesn't have to lead to dystopia. But, left unregulated, it probably will.
claim: Without regulation, AI will probably lead to dystopian outcomes

trigger: Casey Newton essay on AI skepticism
quote: If there is even a 5% chance I am wrong about diminishing returns, we still ought to be moving immediately to prepare
claim: Even with a low probability that LLM scaling will continue to improve rapidly, society should immediately prepare for powerful AI through regulation

trigger: Casey Newton essay on AI skepticism
quote: we still really cannot guarantee that any given system will be honest, harmless, or helpful, rather than sycophantic, dishonest, toxic or biased
claim: Current AI systems cannot be guaranteed to be honest, harmless, or helpful rather than sycophantic, dishonest, toxic, or biased

trigger: Casey Newton essay on AI skepticism
quote: AI needs a dedicated regulator
claim: AI needs a dedicated government regulatory body

trigger: Casey Newton essay on AI skepticism
quote: the whole ballgame is whether the $200+B investment in Generative AI is the right path to AI or not
claim: The central question in AI is whether the $200+ billion investment in generative AI is the right path to AI

trigger: Casey Newton essay on AI skepticism
quote: thinking that if an AI is stupid (or overrated) it can't be dangerous. As I have written multiple times before this is not so
claim: AI systems can be both limited/overrated and simultaneously dangerous; stupidity and danger are not mutually exclusive

trigger: Casey Newton essay on AI skepticism
quote: a stupid AI that is blind to facts can be misused to create propaganda, or create deepfakes
claim: Even unreliable AI systems can be misused to create propaganda and deepfakes

trigger: Casey Newton essay on AI skepticism
quote: we will get to AGI, but the field has been pursuing a hypothesis that was basically (just as I anticipated) disproven, so we will need new ideas
claim: AGI will eventually be achieved but will require new ideas beyond current LLM approaches, which have been shown to have diminishing returns

trigger: Casey Newton essay on AI skepticism
quote: LLM's in particular are wildly overrated
claim: LLMs are wildly overrated relative to what has been promised of them
